mnemonics.title: NDP - New Delete Priority
mnemonics.content: Remember: Global new/delete can be overridden by class-specific versions - Class-specific takes priority (C) over Global (G): G < C in priority order. Think: C comes after G alphabetically, so it has higher priority.

mnemonics.title: DAN - Delete Array Note
mnemonics.content: Always match new[] with delete[] - The extra brackets [] indicate array deletion. Think: A for Array = Always Add Array brackets.

mnemonics.title: RACE - Resource Acquisition Cleanup Entry
mnemonics.content: RAII: Constructor Acquires (A), Destructor Releases (R) - Both happen at scope entry/exit automatically.

mnemonics.title: NSP - New Size Parameter
mnemonics.content: New needs Size: operator new(size_t size) - Remember the 'S' in new stands for Size. Always takes size_t as first parameter.

mnemonics.title: SPU - Smart Pointer Usage
mnemonics.content: Use unique_ptr for single objects, shared_ptr for shared ownership - u for unique, s for shared. Automates delete.

acronyms.usage: C++ technique where resources are acquired in constructor and released in destructor for automatic cleanup.
Mechanism that allows type of object to be determined at runtime, used with dynamic_cast.
C++ library providing containers, algorithms, and iterators for memory management.
Idiom to hide implementation details using opaque pointer, often used with custom allocators.
C++17 feature allowing compiler to deduce template arguments from constructor calls.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flowchart: operator new(size) → allocate bytes → call constructor → return pointer. Reverse for delete: call destructor → operator delete(ptr) → free bytes.
Draw memory diagram: Stack grows down (addresses decrease), Heap grows up. Stack: automatic cleanup, Heap: manual (or smart pointer). Mark stack at top, heap at bottom.
Show memory blocks as rectangles. Fill some, leave gaps between. Large request fails because available space is fragmented. Show how pooling fixes this.
Draw pre-allocated buffer as rectangle. Show arrow pointing to middle. Place 'new (buffer) int(42)' inside that location. Shows object constructed at specific address.

analogies.analogy: Like booking a hotel room - operator new reserves the space (memory), then the constructor checks in (initializes) the guest (data).
Like checking out and freeing the room - destructor says goodbye, operator delete releases the room for others.
Like taking a book from library and never returning it - the book (memory) is marked as 'checked out' forever, other people (programs) can't use it.
Like having a librarian (smart pointer) who automatically returns the book when you're done - you don't have to remember to return it.
Like a hotel having all rooms on one floor reserved for长期 guests - faster check-in/out, no searching for available rooms.
